segments.text: Hi there. My name is Lauren Hanford and
I'm the director of product excellence
at Sonar. Uh I'm the moderator for our
session today, the evolved SDLC, how
Cisco scales quality for thousands of
developers.
Joining us today is Steven Burns,
distinguished engineer of AI first
engineering at Cisco. We are going to
have an exciting discussion about how
Cisco uses different approaches to
maintain a system of checks and balances
ensuring all code whether human written
or AI generated meets rigorous security
and reliability standards before it ever
reaches a poll request.
So Stephen, give us some background on
how you started. tell us about your
role, your day-to-day, and your teams at
Cisco.
>> Yeah, so I think uh it's been almost two
years since I've been at Cisco. Um
probably about 6 months into joining. Uh
my leader Rich asked me if I'd like to
take over the uh engineering
productivity team. Um so yeah, I mean I
accepted the role. Cisco has so many
engineers that uh it seemed like a great
opportunity to add value. Um yeah. Uh so
within engineering productivity at that
time uh we really had three teams. Uh so
the first one was quality engineering
and so quality engineering obviously um
you know quality assurance was a big
part of that. So trying to improve the
maturity of quality assurance across
Cisco IT. Um you know introducing things
like golden pathways. Um yeah helping
teams to get better at just testing in
general. But we called it uh Q QE or
quality engineering not quality
assurance because quality of the code
was also a big impact that we wanted the
team to have on Cisco IT. Um so Sonocube
is really a big part of how the team
focuses on the quality of the code
aspect. Um and so uh you know they're
the team that kind of sets the
benchmarks with all of our you know
technical leaders about what what it is
that means you know quality means for us
in Cisco IT.
uh the the second team within um
engineering productivity was engineering
uh data and measurement and so that team
like I guess a lot of large
organizations that I've worked at we
have all the great tools all the
different engineering tools center cube
um being one of them uh but often the
data is not in one place um and it's
difficult for engineering leaders to
really sort of pull out those insights
and easily get access to just what they
need so we formed this team around
collecting all of that data in um one
place building a nice layer for them to
you know um you have to model that data
to make it easier to get out. We created
some dashboards and again that's one way
that we give leaders a view into the
quality of of the code that teams
producing. They can have conversations
about that. Again soda cube is one of
the big sources of that data that we uh
pull in there. And the last team is
engineering experience. So this is sort
of like your classic um DX team. So
developer experience. So, you know,
they're looking for all of the friction
points. Um, you know, the teams have the
right tools, do they have the right
access? You know, um, we run a bunch of
surveys and things like that to gather,
uh, those friction points and things
like that from the teams. Uh, and this
team in particular is the one that
really goes out and um, will work with
teams so that they understand what tools
they have access to. So things like uh
the Sonoc cube uh plugins, you know, the
MCP servers and they'll also develop
tools and things like that uh to just
help the teams address tech debt and
things like that. So we've built a an
agent that we call Koda that works with
SonoCube and help helps teams really
stay on top of things. So they're the
three teams that we have um and uh where
we started.
>> That's great. Yeah. And I really
appreciated um what you shared about KOD
with me the other day on the call and
you know just wanting to hear more about
what it was like to join this
organization right in the midst of this
massive AI transformation in industry
and and how that's impacted your work.
>> Yeah. Well, I mean when I started we we
the team was called engineering
productivity and our focus was on you
know all of the traditional things that
you would think from a DX type
organization.
uh I mean certainly AI was a prominent
part of the strategy but it wasn't sort
of the you know the the the the
cornerstone of the whole strategy and so
it didn't take long I guess as we were
seeing like these models improving the
tools improving and that sort of thing
that we decided yeah really this is the
centerpiece of the strategy uh and
that's when we basically rebadged the
team to be called uh AI first
engineering
um I mean we do all the same things that
we um always used to do but then we've
added this this sort of AI first focus
on top of that. So we went ahead and um
what we've done within AF first
engineering is established you know this
um confluence presence um where we we
publish a lot of how how to get started
uh with AI first engineering u playbooks
um tools best practices we have a
technology radar uh all that sort of
thing um to help teams get moving uh we
also established a webex uh space with
uh all of Cisco IT in that originally um
and that's grown outside of Cisco IT now
to the product uh engineering
organizations just organically. So I
think it's about 4,000 people now. I
think it's growing at about 50 people a
week at the moment. So it's getting hard
to stay on top of the space. Um and the
last thing we did that's probably one of
the more important things was we
established a monthly guild. Um so we
get about I think around 500 people
every month in the guild. We try and
bring uh an external speaker. So it
could be someone from Sonocarcube, you
know, a vendor, it could be some name in
the industry that, you know, talks a lot
about this sort of thing. And then we
have an uh an internal speaker and that
sort of reinforces that these things are
really actually achievable within the
company. It's not just things outside
that people are talking about. So yeah,
that's been the big push u from
engineering productivity uh onto AI
first engineering and now we're starting
to talk about agentic engineering and
maybe AI native engineering as a as a
next step.
That's interesting. I there's so many
questions I have out of that. Um, one I
would love to know, I just want to make
a comment. I guess it seems like you
have a very eager engineering force to
hop into these guilds and sharing ideas.
>> Um, what do you think is kind of a first
domino that maybe created that effect
for folks that are watching here on our
summit today? Um,
>> I mean, I think it's a few things. So,
AI is a big deal for Cisco uh, in
general. So, you know, there's a focus
really from the top down on using AI to,
you know, as a as a productivity
enabler, as a tool for everybody to get
benefit from. Uh, and I think just in
general, there's lots of interest from
the the bottom up as well on trying to
use these tools. And I guess we sit in
the middle trying to make sure that we,
you know, can connect people to the
right tools and unblock them and that
sort of thing. And I think the space has
been really popular and the guild's been
really popular because it's it's one
place within Cisco which is an enormous
company where people can come together
and you know and share what they're
working on and you know maybe talk to
people that they would normally never
talk to from a different part of the
organization. So that's that's really
helped a lot I think.
>> Yeah, it's interesting. It reminds me a
little bit of almost like how open
source creates this community and so
then you have these guilds creating a
lot of community and there begins to be
this real excitement about building
again.
>> Yeah. Yeah. I mean even from the start
engineering productivity we uh you know
set up a bunch of chapters and guilds
and things like that around the normal
sort of um you know different um you
know a backend guild, a front-end guild,
a DevOps guild and that sort of thing.
We didn't get as much uh I guess
interest. I mean there's still talk but
>> yeah this one has really sort of taken
off. Um but I think it's a combination
of all of those things and you know we
go to a lot of effort as well to make
sure that that there is interesting
content. Uh you know I monitor the
space. I look for people that are
sharing yeah you know interesting ideas
and things like that where they've
actually achieved something that seems
really good and so I'll reach out to
them and ask them if they would like to
write a a blog or you know do a little
video that we can share in the space and
that sort of thing. And it I think just
keeping the you know that content sort
of interesting but it's it's their
peers. It's not just outside people
saying this is what could be possible.
It's you're seeing your peers actually
do it which makes a big difference.
>> Yeah that's right. Yeah. Being in that
same context, same set of constraints
and conditions. I can understand that.
>> Yeah.
>> So I want to talk to you about quality
as table stakes and moving that to
quality as an engineering accelerator
and enabler. Um, can you talk to me
about how you see quality today and how
that's changed over the last 18 months,
two years?
>> Yeah, I mean, I think when I joined
Cisco, obviously there's a focus on
quality, but it was that sort of table
stakes. you know, everybody knew they
needed to do it and they'll they'll keep
it at a certain level. But I guess what
what we've tried to do, I guess, is sort
of double down on that and really sort
of attack the tech debt with the tools
that we have now with AI and providing
context to them from Sar Cube and other
tools uh to really sort of pay down that
debt. And if we can do that, um, we're
not just keeping quality high, but we're
actually able to go faster because we've
cleared a lot of that tech debt that's
been there for some time.
>> So that's that's kind of been the big
push from us. Yeah. And
>> do you think that um creates a better
sort of space for even agents to begin
to operate in more and more
autonomously? Do you see that as playing
a role there?
>> Yeah, for sure. And so I guess the way
we approached it was,
you know, a few different layers. So
we've we've tried to kind of attack each
layer really. Uh so we have um teams
that are addressing the tech debt as a
sort of co-pilot assisted kind of model
and uh yeah initially we sort of
provided that context to them through
IDE plugins and things like that so that
they can have that information provided
to their agent. the agent could then
build plans and things like that to
address that that tech debt quite
quickly as things like uh the MCP
servers came online then we could
provide those not even through the ID
plugins but directly to you know the
coding agent uh just as an MCP server
we um we as I said before we built our
own coding agent called koda and so kod
was a little bit different so we we
always had this idea of this sort of
iceberg concept so teams are always
focused on you know the part above the
water because that's what everybody sees
the functional delivery you know the
product owners want these features you
there's certain deadlines we need to hit
that but there's I guess if you've been
a software engineer you know there's a
big part under the water that not
everybody sees but it's really important
stuff so you know uh this is things like
addressing tech debt like keeping things
um patched you know upgrading your
dependencies making sure you've got
quality tests and all that sort of thing
yeah and sometimes that can yeah you be
a little invisible. So what what we
wanted to do was um with Koda was not
just to have people with a an assistant
like Copilot GitHub copilot but to give
them almost an additional teammate. So
Kod's not sitting next to you. KOD's
sort of like a remote employee working
away in the background that you can
assign work to. Um yeah, we set up KOD
so that it's actually a user within um
Jira. So it's just like a normal staff
member. You can um as a team member, you
can go into Jira. You can create a story
just like you would for anybody else. Uh
you can direct coder to which particular
sonar cube instance we're we're worried
about, which particular repository uh
what kind of um tech issues we're
interested in. Uh and basically just
assign it to coder. So Koda will you
know wake up I guess when that that's
assigned to KOD uh it'll go to Sono cube
pull out all the details of that
particular type of incident or that
issue uh it'll develop a plan and it'll
just start working away like a normal
human staff member updating Jura. So
you'll see comments from coder coming in
about what it's doing. Eventually it'll
complete its work. um it'll you know
mark the story as you know sort of like
ready for testing and that sort of thing
and it'll generate a pull request uh
just like a normal uh staff member as
well. Uh and then the last thing we've
started doing recently is
trying to move to more of a a structured
method I guess for how teams are working
with some of the coding agents. Um so
where some of the teams are starting to
move away from actually writing the code
themselves. So we're looking at can we
um inject uh you know Sonar Cube uh
issues into the context of the plans and
things those teams are creating so that
when the agents running it knows that
you know it needs to address these
issues or make sure that we didn't
create any additional issues as part of
its its execution of the plan. So we
kind of try and attack all of the
different levels. Different teams are at
different stages of I guess AI first
engineering or AI native engineering
depending on how you look at it. So we
kind of have options for all of them but
code is there as that additional
teammate um uh just to you get through
that work that it's just a slog that you
know it needs to be done. It doesn't
really require a huge amount of human
input. Um, so just set coder off and get
them to do that and then the, you know,
the actual developers can focus on the
things that do need human input and
decision-m and all of that kind of
thing.
>> Yeah, it must have been quite a magic
moment when that worked the first time.
>> Yeah, I mean KOD I mean I think you hear
this a bit these days. It's sort of like
um it's maybe I think it was like the
third or fourth rewrite we were sort of
on where we you know we learned a lot
the first time and then we did it again
the second time. And initially we
started off trying to address different
kinds of um sonic cube issues in the
same story. And then what we found was
it's better if we just tackle all of the
issues of a certain type and then yeah
we'll have a separate story for the next
one the next one. And that's um that got
us a lot better results. Yeah.
>> I saw a post the other day on LinkedIn
from Rich Disser, one of your teammates,
your leader. Um, and he was talking
about how tech debt zero is within the
realm of possibility right now. And that
is a a very enticing idea, I'm sure, to
a lot of our audience here today. So, I
wonder if you could go into more detail
about that. What did it look like to uh
unleash KOD on that tech debt? Uh, any
other things that you learned along the
way?
>> Yeah, so that that that particular one
was a uh a large program that we were
starting up last year. Cisco, not
necessarily my team. Um,
making some changes to how we interact
with partners and things like that. And
partners are a big deal at Cisco. And it
was a large program. We wanted to make
sure it was successful. Uh, so what we
did um from my team's perspective is we
rotated someone into the team. So we
took a a team member from my team and
just made them part of their team
basically. So rather than sort of us
meeting with them occasionally and
sharing ideas and tools and practices,
we just said you can have a staff member
for you know I think it was three months
or something like that to really sort of
get things uh you know the the
foundations in place and sort of all of
these techniques adopted so that they
sort of launched the program with the
best possible start.
Um and yeah so basically he went into
that team and he did a lot of the things
that we were just talking about. So, you
know, he made sure that all the
engineers um and this was last year, so
some of these newer techniques hadn't
sort of come out yet, but yeah, he made
sure all the engineers had the IDE
plugin. Um they all had um so Copilot is
our sort of core coding agent. We offer
all the different coding agents to
people, but that's the main one that we
offer and it satisfies most people. Um,
so he made sure they were all set up
with that and that there was a way
basically for Copilot to understand the
um the tech issues from the the plugin
that um you know Sono Cube was finding
and basically he just you know kind of
created a way of working with that team
that they were able to just tackle these
issues really rapidly and so they would
just you know identify the tech debt
they wanted to go after get copilot to
make a plan for how to attack that and
that would do it and so essentially I
think um yeah they got through a vast
number of um tech dead issues. I think
it was something like 20 27,000 issues
um and essentially down to zero in yeah
in that 3-month period I think it was
that that yeah that particular staff
member was there. I mean it's always a
you know you're always sort of battling
against keeping it down low and that
sort of thing. But um yeah, I mean there
were a number of other things he did as
well within that team to really set them
up on the right foot, but that was one
of the big pieces that really um helped
out a lot. I think um
once they were set up, he came back into
the team and went back to working on
some of the internal things. But um
yeah, I think that project just um
recently went um live. Uh was quite
successful. I'm sure there were lots of
reasons why it was successful, but um
you know I guess we like to think that
we helped out at the start with um you
know setting up those foundations and
addressing that tech debt so that
essentially that could go fast um from
the start of the program.
>> Yeah, absolutely. I was as you were
describing that I was thinking I wonder
if you are seeing any quantitative or
just qualitative evidence at this point
that you're seeing those those speed up
gains.
Yeah, I mean in a number of teams I
guess we are seeing I mean across the
board we're seeing increases. It varies
a little bit and that sort of things. Uh
but yeah, Rich that you mentioned
before, you know, he'll often talk about
three times uh productivity gains that
we're getting in some teams.
>> Um which is great. You know, we're
seeing the velocity go up and and that
sort of thing. So, you know, the I mean,
we measure productivity more than just
velocity. We measure across, you know,
this speed in terms of PRs. We measure
across uh you know different quality
aspects you know the effectiveness
in a sense are the engineers happy as
well so are we just going fast and
they're not happy um and uh the impact
as well so we you know spending the
majority of our time on uh new features
and that sort of thing rather than
keeping the lights on we kind of try and
balance out those four which actually
comes from the DX core 4 which we don't
necessarily use the platform but we do
use the research from that team and so
We really like that research because
they call it oppositional metrics. So
you can't just, you know, focus on one
at the expense of another. Um, so yeah,
tracking velocity and then those four
things is is a way that I guess we see
that we're all heading in the right
direction in a I guess you can call it
sort of a safe way or a sustainable way.
>> I was going to ask you too about
developer happiness. Anything you would
say on that, the changes you've seen
there for our audience?
>> It's an interesting one. I think more so
lately it's becoming a bit more
polarizing I would say in the sense uh
you know and Rich and I have a different
talk uh about um sort of identity crisis
that engineers are going to go through
and so yeah a lot of engineers including
myself when I was a you hands-on
engineer you're a master of the syntax
um and you know you pride yourself on
the the craftsmanship or the the quality
of the code that you're producing.
I guess as AI,
you know, becomes more and more
prevalent, you're as an engineer getting
further and further away from the code
and if that if your identity was
attached to that, what does that mean?
What's your value? And I think that
there's still a lot of value, but it's
just different. Um, so you you're more,
you know, abstracted to an extent. Um,
but yeah, I guess I'm seeing some people
I guess are sort of possibly not liking
moving away from the code. And then
other people I'm seeing are they
probably had all these ideas built up in
their head for the last 5 years and they
can finally get them all out and they're
working not because we're asking them
to, but sort of, you know, um, crazy
hours working on all of these projects
>> that they just wanted to get done and
now they can. Um, so it's Yeah, it's
sort of almost like two camps I guess
we're seeing.
>> Yeah. Mhm. Well, and I'm sure the guilds
again are helping kind of create those
bridges between two camps or just giving
people on-ramps to to start to figure
this out. I mean, we're all kind of
figuring out in in real time.
>> Yeah, exactly. Yeah. A lot of the
demonstrations, I mean, we use a lot of
videos and things like that that we'll
share. So, you know, some of my tech
leads or solution architects within the
team will do do a demonstration of um,
you know, and sometimes it's not even
planned. It's just they were doing
something themselves and they sort of
had this epiphany.
I can connect this, you know, it might
be sonar cube to this thing to then to
Confluence or something else with an LLM
and I can do this thing that used to
take me hours and now I can do it in,
you know, a couple of minutes and
they'll create a video and just share it
just as examples of um yeah, the kind of
things you can start to do that maybe
not everyone's thinking about or hadn't
made the connection yet or all that sort
of thing.
>> Yeah, just creating ideas and
possibilities is so cheap now, you know,
relatively speaking. It's it's an
exciting era.
>> Yeah, it is. So we've talked about the
move from more legacy workflows to uh AI
augmented workflows.
>> Um you mentioned earlier this move to
more autonomous agentic workflows. Talk
to me about what what that looks like at
Cisco.
>> Yeah. So you know as I was saying
earlier we sort of started off like
everybody else just um with that sort of
um you know co-pilot assisted mode where
it's sitting there beside you and maybe
it's giving you a few tips and things
like that. Um yeah and then we've we we
have coder which is working in the
background. But what we're trying to get
to now is how can we get um engineers to
that next level where you know they
really have stepped away from typing the
code themselves and they're really kind
of conducting this uh group of agents.
Um and so I guess what we're finding is
that uh it really helps to have a a
structured workflow. Uh and so as part
of that we're um really adopting this um
yeah sort of uh investigate um or
research uh then plan uh and then
implement um so what that what that
looks like in reality depending it
doesn't really matter what tool you're
using it could be copilot could be cloud
code doesn't matter but you know as an
engineer you'll have a feature you need
to implement you'll spend some time with
the agent just giving it context and So
the context could be anything from you
here's my son cube issues. It could be
here's the relevant parts of the
codebase that I want you to look at. I
mean it'll do a lot of looking itself
but you can point it specifically at
what you want. You can direct it to
could be API documentation or or really
anything that's going to help with the
feature that you're about to plan. Um I
mean often I'll find myself maybe just
getting lazy even where I'll just take
screenshots and just say I want it to
look like this. And you know, even one
of my um tech leads, we were doing a an
API and he just drew the database table
on a like essentially like a napkin
really. And yeah, if you just upload
that as well. So you kind of giving it
the context. Uh and then once once we've
you know established that sort of
context and the research that we want to
do with the agent, we could go into a
planning mode. So we'll go into a
conversation with the agent about, you
know, this is kind of the feature I'm
trying to develop. Let's have some
backwards and forwards. what are the key
decisions we need to make? Um what extra
information do I need to provide? Uh and
the the tools are getting quite
sophisticated about developing out those
plans. Uh and normally what we're
encouraging uh now as well is once you
get to the end of that planning
conversation, write that plan to a a
markdown document and then establish a
new coding session or a chat session
with a clear context window so that the
agent's got its full sort of brain and
memory available.
>> It's coffee break.
>> Yeah. Yeah, it's ready to go. It's like
the morning. Um,
>> and just basically ask it to implement
the plan. And so we sort of go through
that loop. And now the plan will have in
it verification checks and things like
that. So
yeah, my interpretation I guess some of
the earlier you like if we were looking
at what we were doing a year ago,
creating maybe an API fairly simple CRUD
API, it would work, sometimes it
wouldn't work. Um, sometimes it wouldn't
compile and that sort of thing. But it
seemed like it was sort of like almost
like a oneshot kind of thing. Uh and
whatever you got at the end of that was
what you got. Um I guess what we're
seeing now is the agents looping a lot
more and going through these checklists
with the to-do lists and the
verification checks. And so that's where
we can build in things like our testing
or um you know our son checks and things
like that. So it's you know that maybe
that loop's not finished until uh any
sort of uh issues code qualitywise were
addressed as well. Mhm.
>> So that's kind of the model we're trying
to get to. Um yeah, and really building
in those those checks as part of the
plan. It's not just build the
functionality, it's like what are the
success criteria for exiting.
>> Yeah. And will that help uh sort of the
human burden or human cost of code
review at the end of that? So you the
more that you can build that in in an
automated way, the the
>> Yeah. I mean that that definitely helps
with the initial implementation. So
you'll have a lot less problems when you
exit that. Um, what we find, um, you
know, I guess we're still working out
what works best and that sort of thing,
but,
uh, we'll often ask the agent to do a
code review of the code we just
developed. Sometimes, you know, we might
use different algorithms even to, um,
you get slightly different results and
that sort of thing. You wouldn't ask the
same person to review their own code.
Uh, and then yes, we've got those
verification checks that have passed. we
may have done another sort of pre-code
review and eventually we'll submit a
pull request. And what we recommend for
all of our engineers uh is that they
also use an initial um automated uh pull
request code review as soon as it's
built. So essentially AI wrote the code.
AI may have done a um an initial check
of the code before we submitted the pull
request. Then when they submit the pull
request, you'll have another code
review. Uh and that may generate things
back to the user or the engineer. Uh and
and the whole idea is in that case is
just freeing up your your tech talent
basically like the the the people that
you're paying a lot of money to really
focus on the actual design issues or the
you know the the bigger issues, not the
low-level stuff. Just let
>> AI take care of that basically.
>> Yeah. Everything that you can't sort of
give an instruction set to go do all the
creativity and and all that. Okay.
>> It sounds like a pretty exciting future
with these autonomous agents um coming
from Sonar. I have to ask you uh are you
do you have concerns at all about um
trading off quality security um as you
have more and more autonomous agents as
the code volume and velocity increases?
Um any thoughts on that?
>> Yeah, I mean it's something that comes
up a lot and I guess you see a lot of
horror stories and that sort of thing in
the industry. Uh so we uh yeah we do
track a number of metrics. One of those
metrics is um obviously velocity but the
other ones we track that are important
are defect density and so sometimes
people use different ways to measure
that. For for us defect density means
the uh number of defects per 100 story
points delivered. Um so yeah we can sort
of express that as a percentage. Um and
I guess what we're looking for in that
case is velocity uh increasing but
defect density staying the same. Um you
know so therefore we're delivering more
but we're not reducing quality in terms
of defects. Uh and then we can use
sonorq pass rates you know the issues
and that sort of thing to get an
indication of is the code quality
perhaps getting worse but defects are
staying the same. So we kind of monitor
all of those things to give us an idea.
Uh and what what we uh do as well is we
normally do a check-in every quarter

Um yeah and combination of to my

yeah you um improve significantly. So,
as I said, it's I don't think it's

velocity. It does make it easier like we

and things like that if you can give it

tools. So, I think that's helping with
the quality. And then there is an aspect

I think there's these other testing

think is
is what's really driving the

>> Yeah. Yeah, I mean it's it's encouraging

investment in code as um you know almost
IP or code as a really important um

a focus on
uh making the code bases AI ready in

MD files that describe a lot more about

>> Yeah.
>> It is a forcing function in that way.

>> Yeah. We're definitely seeing that. We
we're trying to actually introduce some

uh to try and take a codebase and you

some decent documentation and things

Um I don't know if you've seen teams

mean the process we went through was

yeah you know as I said my quality

Uh and we all agreed to that. The the

fullText: Hi there. My name is Lauren Hanford and I'm the director of product excellence at Sonar. Uh I'm the moderator for our session today, the evolved SDLC, how Cisco scales quality for thousands of developers. Joining us today is Steven Burns, distinguished engineer of AI first engineering at Cisco. We are going to have an exciting discussion about how Cisco uses different approaches to maintain a system of checks and balances ensuring all code whether human written or AI generated meets rigorous security and reliability standards before it ever reaches a poll request. So Stephen, give us some background on how you started. tell us about your role, your day-to-day, and your teams at Cisco. >> Yeah, so I think uh it's been almost two years since I've been at Cisco. Um probably about 6 months into joining. Uh my leader Rich asked me if I'd like to take over the uh engineering productivity team. Um so yeah, I mean I accepted the role. Cisco has so many engineers that uh it seemed like a great opportunity to add value. Um yeah. Uh so within engineering productivity at that time uh we really had three teams. Uh so the first one was quality engineering and so quality engineering obviously um you know quality assurance was a big part of that. So trying to improve the maturity of quality assurance across Cisco IT. Um you know introducing things like golden pathways. Um yeah helping teams to get better at just testing in general. But we called it uh Q QE or quality engineering not quality assurance because quality of the code was also a big impact that we wanted the team to have on Cisco IT. Um so Sonocube is really a big part of how the team focuses on the quality of the code aspect. Um and so uh you know they're the team that kind of sets the benchmarks with all of our you know technical leaders about what what it is that means you know quality means for us in Cisco IT. uh the the second team within um engineering productivity was engineering uh data and measurement and so that team like I guess a lot of large organizations that I've worked at we have all the great tools all the different engineering tools center cube um being one of them uh but often the data is not in one place um and it's difficult for engineering leaders to really sort of pull out those insights and easily get access to just what they need so we formed this team around collecting all of that data in um one place building a nice layer for them to you know um you have to model that data to make it easier to get out. We created some dashboards and again that's one way that we give leaders a view into the quality of of the code that teams producing. They can have conversations about that. Again soda cube is one of the big sources of that data that we uh pull in there. And the last team is engineering experience. So this is sort of like your classic um DX team. So developer experience. So, you know, they're looking for all of the friction points. Um, you know, the teams have the right tools, do they have the right access? You know, um, we run a bunch of surveys and things like that to gather, uh, those friction points and things like that from the teams. Uh, and this team in particular is the one that really goes out and um, will work with teams so that they understand what tools they have access to. So things like uh the Sonoc cube uh plugins, you know, the MCP servers and they'll also develop tools and things like that uh to just help the teams address tech debt and things like that. So we've built a an agent that we call Koda that works with SonoCube and help helps teams really stay on top of things. So they're the three teams that we have um and uh where we started. >> That's great. Yeah. And I really appreciated um what you shared about KOD with me the other day on the call and you know just wanting to hear more about what it was like to join this organization right in the midst of this massive AI transformation in industry and and how that's impacted your work. >> Yeah. Well, I mean when I started we we the team was called engineering productivity and our focus was on you know all of the traditional things that you would think from a DX type organization. uh I mean certainly AI was a prominent part of the strategy but it wasn't sort of the you know the the the the cornerstone of the whole strategy and so it didn't take long I guess as we were seeing like these models improving the tools improving and that sort of thing that we decided yeah really this is the centerpiece of the strategy uh and that's when we basically rebadged the team to be called uh AI first engineering um I mean we do all the same things that we um always used to do but then we've added this this sort of AI first focus on top of that. So we went ahead and um what we've done within AF first engineering is established you know this um confluence presence um where we we publish a lot of how how to get started uh with AI first engineering u playbooks um tools best practices we have a technology radar uh all that sort of thing um to help teams get moving uh we also established a webex uh space with uh all of Cisco IT in that originally um and that's grown outside of Cisco IT now to the product uh engineering organizations just organically. So I think it's about 4,000 people now. I think it's growing at about 50 people a week at the moment. So it's getting hard to stay on top of the space. Um and the last thing we did that's probably one of the more important things was we established a monthly guild. Um so we get about I think around 500 people every month in the guild. We try and bring uh an external speaker. So it could be someone from Sonocarcube, you know, a vendor, it could be some name in the industry that, you know, talks a lot about this sort of thing. And then we have an uh an internal speaker and that sort of reinforces that these things are really actually achievable within the company. It's not just things outside that people are talking about. So yeah, that's been the big push u from engineering productivity uh onto AI first engineering and now we're starting to talk about agentic engineering and maybe AI native engineering as a as a next step. That's interesting. I there's so many questions I have out of that. Um, one I would love to know, I just want to make a comment. I guess it seems like you have a very eager engineering force to hop into these guilds and sharing ideas. >> Um, what do you think is kind of a first domino that maybe created that effect for folks that are watching here on our summit today? Um, >> I mean, I think it's a few things. So, AI is a big deal for Cisco uh, in general. So, you know, there's a focus really from the top down on using AI to, you know, as a as a productivity enabler, as a tool for everybody to get benefit from. Uh, and I think just in general, there's lots of interest from the the bottom up as well on trying to use these tools. And I guess we sit in the middle trying to make sure that we, you know, can connect people to the right tools and unblock them and that sort of thing. And I think the space has been really popular and the guild's been really popular because it's it's one place within Cisco which is an enormous company where people can come together and you know and share what they're working on and you know maybe talk to people that they would normally never talk to from a different part of the organization. So that's that's really helped a lot I think. >> Yeah, it's interesting. It reminds me a little bit of almost like how open source creates this community and so then you have these guilds creating a lot of community and there begins to be this real excitement about building again. >> Yeah. Yeah. I mean even from the start engineering productivity we uh you know set up a bunch of chapters and guilds and things like that around the normal sort of um you know different um you know a backend guild, a front-end guild, a DevOps guild and that sort of thing. We didn't get as much uh I guess interest. I mean there's still talk but >> yeah this one has really sort of taken off. Um but I think it's a combination of all of those things and you know we go to a lot of effort as well to make sure that that there is interesting content. Uh you know I monitor the space. I look for people that are sharing yeah you know interesting ideas and things like that where they've actually achieved something that seems really good and so I'll reach out to them and ask them if they would like to write a a blog or you know do a little video that we can share in the space and that sort of thing. And it I think just keeping the you know that content sort of interesting but it's it's their peers. It's not just outside people saying this is what could be possible. It's you're seeing your peers actually do it which makes a big difference. >> Yeah that's right. Yeah. Being in that same context, same set of constraints and conditions. I can understand that. >> Yeah. >> So I want to talk to you about quality as table stakes and moving that to quality as an engineering accelerator and enabler. Um, can you talk to me about how you see quality today and how that's changed over the last 18 months, two years? >> Yeah, I mean, I think when I joined Cisco, obviously there's a focus on quality, but it was that sort of table stakes. you know, everybody knew they needed to do it and they'll they'll keep it at a certain level. But I guess what what we've tried to do, I guess, is sort of double down on that and really sort of attack the tech debt with the tools that we have now with AI and providing context to them from Sar Cube and other tools uh to really sort of pay down that debt. And if we can do that, um, we're not just keeping quality high, but we're actually able to go faster because we've cleared a lot of that tech debt that's been there for some time. >> So that's that's kind of been the big push from us. Yeah. And >> do you think that um creates a better sort of space for even agents to begin to operate in more and more autonomously? Do you see that as playing a role there? >> Yeah, for sure. And so I guess the way we approached it was, you know, a few different layers. So we've we've tried to kind of attack each layer really. Uh so we have um teams that are addressing the tech debt as a sort of co-pilot assisted kind of model and uh yeah initially we sort of provided that context to them through IDE plugins and things like that so that they can have that information provided to their agent. the agent could then build plans and things like that to address that that tech debt quite quickly as things like uh the MCP servers came online then we could provide those not even through the ID plugins but directly to you know the coding agent uh just as an MCP server we um we as I said before we built our own coding agent called koda and so kod was a little bit different so we we always had this idea of this sort of iceberg concept so teams are always focused on you know the part above the water because that's what everybody sees the functional delivery you know the product owners want these features you there's certain deadlines we need to hit that but there's I guess if you've been a software engineer you know there's a big part under the water that not everybody sees but it's really important stuff so you know uh this is things like addressing tech debt like keeping things um patched you know upgrading your dependencies making sure you've got quality tests and all that sort of thing yeah and sometimes that can yeah you be a little invisible. So what what we wanted to do was um with Koda was not just to have people with a an assistant like Copilot GitHub copilot but to give them almost an additional teammate. So Kod's not sitting next to you. KOD's sort of like a remote employee working away in the background that you can assign work to. Um yeah, we set up KOD so that it's actually a user within um Jira. So it's just like a normal staff member. You can um as a team member, you can go into Jira. You can create a story just like you would for anybody else. Uh you can direct coder to which particular sonar cube instance we're we're worried about, which particular repository uh what kind of um tech issues we're interested in. Uh and basically just assign it to coder. So Koda will you know wake up I guess when that that's assigned to KOD uh it'll go to Sono cube pull out all the details of that particular type of incident or that issue uh it'll develop a plan and it'll just start working away like a normal human staff member updating Jura. So you'll see comments from coder coming in about what it's doing. Eventually it'll complete its work. um it'll you know mark the story as you know sort of like ready for testing and that sort of thing and it'll generate a pull request uh just like a normal uh staff member as well. Uh and then the last thing we've started doing recently is trying to move to more of a a structured method I guess for how teams are working with some of the coding agents. Um so where some of the teams are starting to move away from actually writing the code themselves. So we're looking at can we um inject uh you know Sonar Cube uh issues into the context of the plans and things those teams are creating so that when the agents running it knows that you know it needs to address these issues or make sure that we didn't create any additional issues as part of its its execution of the plan. So we kind of try and attack all of the different levels. Different teams are at different stages of I guess AI first engineering or AI native engineering depending on how you look at it. So we kind of have options for all of them but code is there as that additional teammate um uh just to you get through that work that it's just a slog that you know it needs to be done. It doesn't really require a huge amount of human input. Um, so just set coder off and get them to do that and then the, you know, the actual developers can focus on the things that do need human input and decision-m and all of that kind of thing. >> Yeah, it must have been quite a magic moment when that worked the first time. >> Yeah, I mean KOD I mean I think you hear this a bit these days. It's sort of like um it's maybe I think it was like the third or fourth rewrite we were sort of on where we you know we learned a lot the first time and then we did it again the second time. And initially we started off trying to address different kinds of um sonic cube issues in the same story. And then what we found was it's better if we just tackle all of the issues of a certain type and then yeah we'll have a separate story for the next one the next one. And that's um that got us a lot better results. Yeah. >> I saw a post the other day on LinkedIn from Rich Disser, one of your teammates, your leader. Um, and he was talking about how tech debt zero is within the realm of possibility right now. And that is a a very enticing idea, I'm sure, to a lot of our audience here today. So, I wonder if you could go into more detail about that. What did it look like to uh unleash KOD on that tech debt? Uh, any other things that you learned along the way? >> Yeah, so that that that particular one was a uh a large program that we were starting up last year. Cisco, not necessarily my team. Um, making some changes to how we interact with partners and things like that. And partners are a big deal at Cisco. And it was a large program. We wanted to make sure it was successful. Uh, so what we did um from my team's perspective is we rotated someone into the team. So we took a a team member from my team and just made them part of their team basically. So rather than sort of us meeting with them occasionally and sharing ideas and tools and practices, we just said you can have a staff member for you know I think it was three months or something like that to really sort of get things uh you know the the foundations in place and sort of all of these techniques adopted so that they sort of launched the program with the best possible start. Um and yeah so basically he went into that team and he did a lot of the things that we were just talking about. So, you know, he made sure that all the engineers um and this was last year, so some of these newer techniques hadn't sort of come out yet, but yeah, he made sure all the engineers had the IDE plugin. Um they all had um so Copilot is our sort of core coding agent. We offer all the different coding agents to people, but that's the main one that we offer and it satisfies most people. Um, so he made sure they were all set up with that and that there was a way basically for Copilot to understand the um the tech issues from the the plugin that um you know Sono Cube was finding and basically he just you know kind of created a way of working with that team that they were able to just tackle these issues really rapidly and so they would just you know identify the tech debt they wanted to go after get copilot to make a plan for how to attack that and that would do it and so essentially I think um yeah they got through a vast number of um tech dead issues. I think it was something like 20 27,000 issues um and essentially down to zero in yeah in that 3-month period I think it was that that yeah that particular staff member was there. I mean it's always a you know you're always sort of battling against keeping it down low and that sort of thing. But um yeah, I mean there were a number of other things he did as well within that team to really set them up on the right foot, but that was one of the big pieces that really um helped out a lot. I think um once they were set up, he came back into the team and went back to working on some of the internal things. But um yeah, I think that project just um recently went um live. Uh was quite successful. I'm sure there were lots of reasons why it was successful, but um you know I guess we like to think that we helped out at the start with um you know setting up those foundations and addressing that tech debt so that essentially that could go fast um from the start of the program. >> Yeah, absolutely. I was as you were describing that I was thinking I wonder if you are seeing any quantitative or just qualitative evidence at this point that you're seeing those those speed up gains. Yeah, I mean in a number of teams I guess we are seeing I mean across the board we're seeing increases. It varies a little bit and that sort of things. Uh but yeah, Rich that you mentioned before, you know, he'll often talk about three times uh productivity gains that we're getting in some teams. >> Um which is great. You know, we're seeing the velocity go up and and that sort of thing. So, you know, the I mean, we measure productivity more than just velocity. We measure across, you know, this speed in terms of PRs. We measure across uh you know different quality aspects you know the effectiveness in a sense are the engineers happy as well so are we just going fast and they're not happy um and uh the impact as well so we you know spending the majority of our time on uh new features and that sort of thing rather than keeping the lights on we kind of try and balance out those four which actually comes from the DX core 4 which we don't necessarily use the platform but we do use the research from that team and so We really like that research because they call it oppositional metrics. So you can't just, you know, focus on one at the expense of another. Um, so yeah, tracking velocity and then those four things is is a way that I guess we see that we're all heading in the right direction in a I guess you can call it sort of a safe way or a sustainable way. >> I was going to ask you too about developer happiness. Anything you would say on that, the changes you've seen there for our audience? >> It's an interesting one. I think more so lately it's becoming a bit more polarizing I would say in the sense uh you know and Rich and I have a different talk uh about um sort of identity crisis that engineers are going to go through and so yeah a lot of engineers including myself when I was a you hands-on engineer you're a master of the syntax um and you know you pride yourself on the the craftsmanship or the the quality of the code that you're producing. I guess as AI, you know, becomes more and more prevalent, you're as an engineer getting further and further away from the code and if that if your identity was attached to that, what does that mean? What's your value? And I think that there's still a lot of value, but it's just different. Um, so you you're more, you know, abstracted to an extent. Um, but yeah, I guess I'm seeing some people I guess are sort of possibly not liking moving away from the code. And then other people I'm seeing are they probably had all these ideas built up in their head for the last 5 years and they can finally get them all out and they're working not because we're asking them to, but sort of, you know, um, crazy hours working on all of these projects >> that they just wanted to get done and now they can. Um, so it's Yeah, it's sort of almost like two camps I guess we're seeing. >> Yeah. Mhm. Well, and I'm sure the guilds again are helping kind of create those bridges between two camps or just giving people on-ramps to to start to figure this out. I mean, we're all kind of figuring out in in real time. >> Yeah, exactly. Yeah. A lot of the demonstrations, I mean, we use a lot of videos and things like that that we'll share. So, you know, some of my tech leads or solution architects within the team will do do a demonstration of um, you know, and sometimes it's not even planned. It's just they were doing something themselves and they sort of had this epiphany. I can connect this, you know, it might be sonar cube to this thing to then to Confluence or something else with an LLM and I can do this thing that used to take me hours and now I can do it in, you know, a couple of minutes and they'll create a video and just share it just as examples of um yeah, the kind of things you can start to do that maybe not everyone's thinking about or hadn't made the connection yet or all that sort of thing. >> Yeah, just creating ideas and possibilities is so cheap now, you know, relatively speaking. It's it's an exciting era. >> Yeah, it is. So we've talked about the move from more legacy workflows to uh AI augmented workflows. >> Um you mentioned earlier this move to more autonomous agentic workflows. Talk to me about what what that looks like at Cisco. >> Yeah. So you know as I was saying earlier we sort of started off like everybody else just um with that sort of um you know co-pilot assisted mode where it's sitting there beside you and maybe it's giving you a few tips and things like that. Um yeah and then we've we we have coder which is working in the background. But what we're trying to get to now is how can we get um engineers to that next level where you know they really have stepped away from typing the code themselves and they're really kind of conducting this uh group of agents. Um and so I guess what we're finding is that uh it really helps to have a a structured workflow.
